refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation while remaining solution-neutral by avoiding prescriptive terms like 'support' (which implies mechanism) and instead focusing on the outcome: maintaining communication integrity. The phrase 'customer-defined packet loss limits' and 'agreed network conditions' are preserved as acceptance criteria anchors without embedding implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profiles, bandwidth constraints) and measure end-to-end packet loss across the communication path. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Log packet loss statistics and any correlation with operator commands, video feed quality, or equipment response latency.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit operator commands from tele-operator to remote equipment with fidelity sufficient to preserve intended control intent.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback from remote equipment to tele-operator with fidelity sufficient to enable safe situational awareness.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication degradation (packet loss, latency anomalies) to the tele-operator and safety manager in real time.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.
DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to customer network conditions, to recover lost packets without retransmission.
DP4: Implement a quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation and enforcement layer that prioritizes critical control commands and telemetry over non-critical data, with dynamic bandwidth allocation.

DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.

potential_sources: Heartbeat monitoring is decoupled from command/feedback payload processing; can be implemented as an independent timing layer.
SACK mechanism is orthogonal to payload encoding; operates at frame level without constraining application logic.
Adaptive timeout logic depends on network metrics (latency, loss rate) but these are observable inputs, not tightly coupled to control logic.
Risk: If timeout adaptation is too aggressive, may cause unnecessary retransmissions; mitigation: parameterize thresholds and expose to customer tuning.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 avoids hard coupling to specific network topologies (unlike DP2's dual-channel assumption) and does not mandate redundancy overhead at all times (unlike DP1/DP3). It allows independent evolution of heartbeat strategy, acknowledgment policy, and retransmission logic. Coupling is primarily through shared network metrics, which is acceptable and necessary.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined packet loss target (≤1% or custom) is measurable and stable during agreed network conditions; Risk: If network conditions are highly variable or unmeasured, adaptive tuning may fail to converge.
Assumption: Teleoperation commands and feedback have distinct criticality levels; Risk: If all frames are equally critical, SACK selectivity provides no benefit and adds complexity.

Assumption: Latency tolerance for retransmission is known (e.g., human operator reaction time ~200–500 ms); Risk: If latency budget is very tight (<100 ms), adaptive retransmission may exceed acceptable delay.

Assumption: Network conditions are characterized before deployment (baseline loss, jitter, bandwidth); Risk: Undocumented network anomalies (e.g., periodic congestion) may cause DP5 to oscillate or fail to meet acceptance criteria.
Assumption: Heartbeat and SACK overhead is acceptable within available bandwidth; Risk: On severely constrained links, monitoring overhead itself may degrade throughput.
Assumption: Customer operational policies permit real-time network monitoring and telemetry collection; Risk: Privacy or security policies may restrict visibility into network metrics.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by detecting and recovering from packet loss within customer-defined limits (target ≤1%) under agreed network conditions, while respecting latency constraints (specify acceptable round-trip delay for command-feedback cycle) and operational policies. DP5 should be paired with explicit IM boundaries: (IM-A) Network Quality Monitor module (measures loss, latency, jitter), (IM-B) Adaptive Retransmission Engine (manages timeouts and SACK logic), (IM-C) Heartbeat & Liveness Detector (independent timing). This decomposition enables independent testing, tuning, and customer configuration of each concern. Add acceptance criteria: (1) Packet loss recovery achieves ≤1% end-to-end loss under test conditions, (2) Command latency remains within [specify] ms, (3) Monitoring overhead ≤[specify]% of available bandwidth.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote control communication protocols, including heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment of critical frames, adaptive timeout-driven retransmission, and latency/reliability trade-off logic for operator-to-vehicle command and feedback channels.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides underlying transport layer; Teleoperation module configures heartbeat intervals, SACK policies, and timeout parameters based on network conditions detected via Network Link.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and mission directives; Teleoperation ensures reliable delivery of critical tasking frames via SACK and adaptive retransmission.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports vehicle state and acknowledgment status; heartbeat monitoring results inform telemetry reporting on link health.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals critical communication failures (e.g., heartbeat loss, timeout exhaustion) that trigger safety interventions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are core teleoperation protocol concerns. Latency/reliability trade-off is inherent to remote control. Minor diffusion: timeout thresholds may need coordination with Safety Services for failsafe behavior.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns vehicle state and actuation, not communication protocol logic. Heartbeat and SACK are operator-side concerns. Placing DP here would scatter protocol logic across vehicle and operator domains.

Moderate fit: Safety Services owns failsafe logic and alert generation, but not the communication protocol itself. Adaptive retransmission and SACK are protocol mechanics, not safety policy. Would require Safety Services to own implementation details outside its domain.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is a top-level container. Placing DP here provides no architectural clarity and forces all subsystems to depend on a monolithic solution-level module. Violates separation of concerns.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it is responsible for managing the communication protocol between operator and vehicle. Heartbeat monitoring detects link liveness, SACK ensures critical commands are reliably received, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission balances latency (operator responsiveness) against reliability (command integrity). All three mechanisms are intrinsic to remote control protocol design and have no meaningful place in Vehicle, Safety Services, or the solution container.
containment_risks: Timeout threshold tuning may require feedback from Safety Services to ensure failsafe semantics (e.g., how long to wait before declaring link loss and triggering emergency stop). Mitigation: define a SafetyAlert interface contract that specifies timeout-to-alert latency requirements.

Heartbeat interval and SACK selectivity may need to adapt based on network conditions detected at the Network Link layer. Mitigation: ensure Teleoperation has read access to Network Link diagnostics (jitter, packet loss, latency estimates) to inform adaptive parameters.

Vehicle-side acknowledgment of receipt (e.g., motor command executed) must be distinguished from operator-side SACK of command transmission. Mitigation: clearly separate operator-to-vehicle protocol (owned by Teleoperation) from vehicle-to-operator telemetry (owned by Vehicle/Telemetry interface).

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns the mechanisms (heartbeat, SACK, adaptive retransmission) that directly control packet loss performance. Verification can be conducted via network simulation, field testing under controlled packet loss injection, and telemetry logging of heartbeat/SACK/retransmission events correlated with observed packet loss rates.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to the Teleoperation module. The DP mechanisms (heartbeat monitoring, SACK, adaptive timeout-driven retransmission) are all protocol-level concerns owned by Teleoperation. The external interfaces (Network Link for transport diagnostics, Tasking for command ingress, Telemetry for health export, SafetyAlert for failsafe signaling) establish the necessary information flows to realize the FR without ambiguity about ownership boundaries.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR. Heartbeat monitoring detects link liveness and enables early detection of packet loss conditions. Selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames ensures operator commands are reliably received despite packet loss. Adaptive timeout-driven retransmission balances latency (operator responsiveness) against reliability (command integrity) by tuning retry behavior based on observed network conditions. The hybrid approach is well-suited to teleoperation where both low latency and high reliability are required. The DP is implementable and testable against the acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1%).

diffusion_risks: Timeout threshold tuning creates a DP<->IM bridge risk: timeout values must be coordinated with Safety Services to ensure failsafe semantics (e.g., how long Teleoperation waits before declaring link loss and triggering SafetyAlert). If timeout thresholds are scattered across Teleoperation and Safety Services without a clear contract, adaptive retransmission logic may conflict with safety failsafe timing. Mitigation: define a formal SafetyAlert interface contract specifying maximum latency from heartbeat loss detection to alert generation, and ensure Teleoperation owns timeout parameter tuning subject to this constraint.

Adaptive parameter selection (heartbeat interval, SACK selectivity, retransmission backoff) depends on real-time network diagnostics from the Network Link layer. If Teleoperation does not have clean read access to Network Link metrics (jitter, packet loss estimates, latency), adaptive logic will be incomplete or require polling/callbacks that blur the IM boundary. Mitigation: establish a Network Link diagnostics interface that exports current network health metrics (packet loss rate, latency percentiles, jitter) that Teleoperation can query to inform adaptive parameter updates.

Vehicle-side acknowledgment (motor command executed) must be clearly separated from operator-side SACK (command transmission received). If vehicle telemetry and operator-side protocol acknowledgments are conflated in the Telemetry interface, Teleoperation may incorrectly interpret vehicle state as command confirmation, leading to premature retransmission suppression. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry interface exports only vehicle state; Teleoperation maintains its own internal SACK/acknowledgment state machine separate from vehicle feedback.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The Teleoperation module is the natural and sole owner of DP5. Heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are intrinsic to remote control protocol design and have no meaningful place in Vehicle (which owns actuation and state), Safety Services (which owns failsafe policy, not protocol mechanics), or the solution container (which provides no architectural clarity). The minor diffusion risk (0.08) is well-managed via the identified external interfaces. No alternative module is a better fit.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the SafetyAlert interface contract: specify the maximum acceptable latency from heartbeat loss detection to SafetyAlert signal generation. This ensures Teleoperation's timeout thresholds are tuned to meet safety requirements without Safety Services needing to own protocol implementation details.

Establish a Network Link diagnostics interface that exports real-time metrics (packet loss rate, latency percentiles, jitter estimates) to Teleoperation. This enables adaptive parameter tuning without requiring Teleoperation to directly poll or depend on Network Link internals.

Document the separation of concerns between operator-side SACK/acknowledgment (owned by Teleoperation) and vehicle-side command execution feedback (owned by Vehicle/Telemetry). Add a note to the Telemetry interface clarifying that it exports vehicle state only, not protocol-level acknowledgments.

Add acceptance test criteria to the FR: (1) Packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions (existing); (2) Heartbeat loss detection latency ≤ [X ms] (to be defined with Safety Services); (3) Adaptive timeout adjustment time ≤ [Y ms] in response to network condition changes (to be defined with Network Link). These criteria make verification concrete and testable.

decision.justification: The FR-DP-IM gates all pass with high confidence (FR_IM containment 0.92, DP directly addresses FR, IM diffusion risks well-identified and mitigable). However, the safety criticality of this FR (high) and the identified diffusion risks—particularly timeout threshold coordination with Safety Services and adaptive parameter tuning dependency on Network Link diagnostics—warrant decomposition of the DP into sub-FRs that explicitly address these critical interfaces and dependencies. Decomposition will make the safety-critical timeout contract and network diagnostics coupling analyzable at the next level, reducing integration risk. The recommended revisions (SafetyAlert interface contract, Network Link diagnostics interface, SACK/vehicle-state separation) are best realized as derived sub-FRs with their own DP-IM mappings, ensuring ownership clarity and testability.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Detect heartbeat loss and signal link failure to Safety Services within [X ms] of heartbeat timeout, enabling failsafe response.
FR5.2: Adapt retransmission timeout and heartbeat interval based on real-time network diagnostics (packet loss rate, latency, jitter) exported by Network Link.
FR5.3: Maintain operator-side SACK/acknowledgment state machine independent of vehicle-side command execution feedback, ensuring protocol-level confirmation is not conflated with vehicle state.

FR5.4: Verify packet loss performance (≤ 1%) under agreed network conditions via simulation, field testing with packet loss injection, and telemetry correlation of heartbeat/SACK/retransmission events.

revise.why: Add explicit acceptance test criteria to FR5 (parent): (1) Packet loss ≤ 1% under agreed network conditions; (2) Heartbeat loss detection latency ≤ [X ms] (to be defined with Safety Services); (3) Adaptive timeout adjustment time ≤ [Y ms] in response to network condition changes (to be defined with Network Link). These criteria make the FR testable and bind the sub-FRs to measurable safety and performance contracts. Also clarify that the FR applies to operator-to-vehicle command path; acknowledge that vehicle-to-operator telemetry may have different reliability requirements (to be addressed in a separate FR if needed).

assumptions: Heartbeat loss detection latency [X ms] will be jointly defined by Teleoperation and Safety Services based on failsafe response time requirements (e.g., if SafetyAlert must trigger within 100 ms of link loss, heartbeat timeout must be ≤ 100 ms minus processing overhead).

Network Link module will provide a diagnostics interface exporting packet loss rate, latency percentiles, and jitter estimates in real-time or near-real-time (latency ≤ [Y ms]) to enable Teleoperation adaptive tuning.

Vehicle-side command execution feedback (e.g., 'motor reached target speed') is distinct from operator-side protocol acknowledgment (e.g., 'command packet received and queued for execution') and will not be conflated in the Telemetry interface.

Adaptive parameter tuning (heartbeat interval, SACK selectivity, retransmission backoff) will be owned and tuned by Teleoperation; Safety Services will not override these parameters but will define the safety contract (e.g., maximum acceptable latency from heartbeat loss to failsafe alert).

Packet loss acceptance criterion (≤ 1%) is achievable under the agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G cellular, WiFi 6, or wired Ethernet with specified packet loss injection rates); if network conditions are worse, the criterion may need revision or the system may require additional redundancy (e.g., dual-link failover).
open_risks: Timeout threshold coordination: If SafetyAlert interface contract is not formalized before DP implementation, Teleoperation may choose timeout values that conflict with safety failsafe timing, leading to either premature failsafe triggers (nuisance alerts) or delayed failsafe response (safety gap). Mitigation: FR5.1 sub-FR explicitly addresses this; define SafetyAlert interface contract in DP5.1 before implementation.

Network diagnostics latency: If Network Link diagnostics interface exports metrics with high latency (e.g., 500 ms old data), Teleoperation's adaptive tuning will be reactive rather than proactive, potentially missing rapid network condition changes. Mitigation: FR5.2 sub-FR requires real-time or near-real-time diagnostics; define acceptable latency in DP5.2.

SACK/vehicle-state conflation: If Telemetry interface is not clearly separated from protocol-level acknowledgments, integration testing may reveal that Teleoperation incorrectly suppresses retransmission based on vehicle state (e.g., 'motor started') rather than protocol confirmation (e.g., 'command packet received'). Mitigation: FR5.3 sub-FR explicitly addresses this; document interface separation in DP5.3 and verify via unit/integration tests.

Adaptive parameter tuning stability: If adaptive timeout and heartbeat interval adjustments are too aggressive (e.g., doubling timeout on first packet loss), the system may oscillate between aggressive and conservative modes, degrading reliability. Mitigation: DP5.2 should specify tuning algorithm (e.g., exponential backoff with bounds, moving average of network metrics) and include stability analysis or simulation validation.

Verification under diverse network conditions: Packet loss ≤ 1% criterion must be verified under agreed network conditions, but field deployment may encounter conditions outside the agreement (e.g., worse packet loss, higher jitter). Mitigation: FR5.4 sub-FR requires simulation and field testing; define acceptance test matrix covering nominal, degraded, and edge-case network conditions.
evidence_needed: SafetyAlert interface specification: maximum latency from heartbeat loss detection to alert generation, alert signal format, and failsafe semantics (e.g., does SafetyAlert trigger immediate motor shutdown or graceful deceleration?).
Network Link diagnostics interface specification: exported metrics (packet loss rate, latency percentiles, jitter), update frequency, and accuracy/confidence bounds.
Teleoperation protocol state machine design: SACK/acknowledgment logic, retransmission algorithm, timeout tuning rules, and separation from vehicle-side feedback.
Simulation model: network conditions (packet loss, latency, jitter), heartbeat/SACK/retransmission behavior, and packet loss rate measurement under various network scenarios.

Field test plan: network conditions to be tested (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, WiFi 6, wired Ethernet with packet loss injection), test duration, success criteria (packet loss ≤ 1%), and telemetry logging for post-test analysis.
Adaptive tuning algorithm specification: timeout adjustment rules, heartbeat interval adjustment rules, bounds on parameter values, and stability analysis or simulation validation.

Integration test cases: SACK/vehicle-state separation, SafetyAlert triggering on heartbeat loss, adaptive parameter adjustment in response to network condition changes, and packet loss measurement under agreed conditions.
